title: HISA02 — Rise of the Machines
text:
Quick links

Week 1 · Week 2 · Week 3 · Week 4 · Week 5 · Week 6 · Week 7
All lectures (PDF)
Syllabus
Assignments

title: Week 3
text:
Two hours this week are lost to Victoria Day – no class on Monday. But for the remaining lectures, we have jumped with both feet into the pool of “modernity.” We will stay with the effects of industrialization: first, Steam, Coal, Industry, and Empire, an introduction to the steam engine and its transformative effects on labour and society; then Railroads, Signals & Telegraphs, exploring the new relationships to geographical space made possible by the steam locomotive and the telegraph.
You will also have Tutorial 3.

title: Week 3
text:
Understanding the Readings

Steam, Coal, Industry, and Empire
The steam engine is one of the most influential technological developments in human history. We will read a brief primary source about the change in working conditions brought on by steam power, as well as an explanation of how steam engines work and their wider social effects.


Railroads, Signals & Telegraphs
In “Railroads, Signals & Telegraphs” we will discuss the new relationships to geographical space that are made possible by the steam locomotive, on the one hand, and the telegraph, on the other. Our main guide here will be Wolfgang Schivelbusch, whose masterful The Railway Journey is one of the classics of “cultural history of technology.”
The most difficult and rewarding reading this week is Schivelbusch’s The Railway Journey. In the 1980’s, Schivelbusch pioneered a new way of writing about the history of technology: a literary, erudite style that indiscriminately mixed the history of artifacts, political change, social transformation, and artistic perception. Of his three books on the emergence of “modernity” (the other two concern electric light and the history of drugs), The Railway Journey is almost certainly the best known.
You may find it helpful to look up some of the 19th century authors he cites, and to ask yourself: do I believe that the changes in perception and in mindset that he describes, can easily be traced back to the proliferation of steam locomotives in the 19th century? That will be a major preoccupation of lecture.

title: Week 3
text:
Tutorial 3 Preparation
You could do worse than to read (reading PDF — access instructions sent over email) with great care. Can you summarize his argument here? How does he make use of metaphor and other literary devices? Are you able to see how this essay stretches the very concept of historical investigation to inquire into phenomena that previously had not been considered the province of history at all? Who are the Wedgwoods and why does Thompson refer to them? And what, especially, do you make of his final sentence:

For there is no such thing as economic growth which is not, at the same time, growth or change of a culture; and the growth of social consciousness, like the growth of a poet’s mind, can never, in the last analysis, be planned.

Can you, meanwhile, understand how the (reading PDF — access instructions sent over email) is relevant to the arguments that Thompson is making? Explain.

title: Week 6
text:
This week we swerve from daily life to the large-scale technologies of warfare and “big science.” We begin with Death from Above: Aerial Bombardment, exploring the ideology of strategic bombing and the experience of being bombed. Then War as Big Tech focusses on the development of “big science” projects in the mid-20th century, particularly the atomic bomb and its legacy. Next, Calculating Machines traces the development of the modern computer industry. Finally, Biology as Big Tech examines how data collection and biological categorization have served as instruments of power.
You will also have Tutorial 6.

title: Week 6
text:
Understanding the Readings

Hippler, “Governing from the Skies”
This is one a number of works by the author on the history of strategic bombing. It may be somewhat difficult to understand if you are not already familiar with the major figures Hippler cites. Don’t worry if you don’t understand everything, but make a real effort. Here are some evocative phrases for you to explore:

“It was in no way surprising, therefore, that air power should have been viewed as a solution to the war of position of 1914-18.” What is this problem, and how does air power offer a solution to it?”
“Designed to restore order, air strikes were no longer a practice of warfare, but rather one of ‘policing’, even ‘imperial policing’: they were practised not within the frontiers of a state, but on a global scale, as a means of governing the world.” What does this mean, and do you agree?
“The evolution of air warfare thus reveals to the world the convergence between the ‘advances’ of international law and the pure violence of the state.” This is Hippler’s closing line. What does it mean?



Survivor Accounts
The two survivor accounts give insight into what it was like to be struck by bombs. Read them both carefully.

Yoshito Matsushige describes the intensity of the Hiroshima blast. He also describes the surreal and horrific experience of wandering among the dead afterwards. What is your response to this text?
Lothar Metzger recounts the horror of the Dresden firebombing. Again, what is your response?
In both cases, the bombing forces were members of “the good guys” – the side fighting against fascism. What is your response to the fact that terrible suffering was intentionally inflicted on civilians by the forces universally acknowledged to be on “the right side of history”?



Bombs and Genomes
This is a textbook chapter and so relatively straightforward. Pay particular attention to the phrase “applied science”; what does the author appear to mean by it? Is their use in tension with the way we have talked about technology up to now?


Uninventing the Bomb
This article from the 1990’s may appear rather quaint, but the question of nuclear weapons expertise remains an extremely current one. McKenzie’s proposed solutions for “uninventing” nuclear weapons are quite different than, say, the approach to that problem that Israel & the US have recently taken.

What avenues for abolition of nuclear weapons does McKenzie identify? Which of them seem plausible to you?
What does “tacit knowledge” mean?
Do you think McKenzie would agree with McClellan that contemporary technology is a form of “applied science”?



Calculating Machines
The 1984 Apple Macintosh ad is an early indicator of the ideological context in which personal computers were introduced into the lives of American consumers. Watch the ad, then ask yourself:

What do personal computers have to do with resisting authoritarian governance?
Suppose you were in charge of crafting a counterargument to the implicit narrative of the ad. What arguments would you make?

The ad contains references to George Orwell’s classic dystopian novel.


Biology as Big Tech
In this lecture, we pick up on our earlier conversation about census-taking and technologies of administration, but now emphasize the exponential increase in data processing power that characterizes the late twentieth and early twenty-first century. Re-read Cohn’s article with fresh eyes: how would you translate his arguments to present-day data collection? How much of his analysis is still current?

title: Week 6
text:
Tutorial 6 Preparation
Be prepared to discuss three topics:

First, think about the readings on aerial bombardment and nuclear weapons. If nuclear weapons are “technological systems”, is bombardment also such a system? What implications does that have? How can we, as ordinary people, mitigate the harms caused by these weapons and the social structures that surround them? Consider how the technology of warfare has moved from the battlefield to the laboratory and the factory – and what that means for the relationship between war and civilian life.
Second, this week traces a path from wartime “big science” (the Manhattan Project, military computing) to the peacetime “big tech” of personal computers and biological data. How does the wartime model of massive, state-funded research shape the technologies we use today? McKenzie asks whether we can “uninvent” nuclear weapons; can we uninvent any technology? What does the Apple Macintosh ad’s vision of liberation through computing look like when set against the history of military-funded research that made personal computers possible?
Also, now is the time to think about the final paper.

title: Week 5
text:
This week we explore the technologies of daily life and energy. We begin with Technologies of Food, using the pressure cooker as a case study in kitchen innovation. Then we turn to Domestic Technology and the Consumption Junction, examining how household technologies transformed family life and gender roles. Next comes Petroleum, Empire, and the Freedom of the Road, exploring how the automobile and the oil industry reshaped societies and environments. Finally, we consider the ongoing Energy Transition and the question of moving beyond carbon.
You will also have Tutorial 5.

title: Week 5
text:
Understanding the Readings

Technologies of Food
This lecture takes a detailed look at cooking technology, using the pressure cooker as a case study. Your main task is to examine the terrific primary sources provided by Prof. deSouza in the (reading PDF — access instructions sent over email). Please examine them in detail.


Domestic Technology and The Consumption Junction
We will read a chapter of Ruth Schwartz Cowan’s influential More Work for Mother and explore her concept of “The Consumption Junction.”
Cowan’s central argument challenges common assumptions about household technology: rather than reducing women’s work, many domestic innovations actually increased it, while changing its character. The “consumption junction” refers to the crucial moment when consumers decide whether and how to adopt new technologies.
Key questions for the readings:

How did the introduction of gas, electricity, and running water transform domestic life?
Why did some labor-saving devices actually create more work for women?
How do social expectations and gender roles shape the adoption of domestic technologies?
What is the “consumption junction” and why is it important for understanding technological change?



Petroleum, Empire, and the Freedom of the Road
We examine one of the most transformative technologies of the 20th century: the automobile and the petroleum industry that powers it. We will read McCarthy’s account of how the automobile industry developed and transformed American society and environment.
Key questions to consider as you read:

How did the automobile industry emerge and what were its early challenges?
What role did petroleum play in reshaping global politics and economics?
How did cars change patterns of urban and suburban development?
What environmental consequences followed from mass automobile adoption?
How did the automobile become associated with ideas of freedom and individualism?
What groups were excluded from or harmed by automobile-centered development?



Energy Transition and the End of Carbon
Details for this lecture are forthcoming. We will explore the history and future of the transition away from carbon-based energy systems.

title: Week 5
text:
Tutorial 5 Preparation
This week’s lectures span a remarkable range of energy and domestic technologies. In tutorial, we will bring them together under the theme of energy and society. Some questions to consider:

How do energy systems – coal, petroleum, electricity – shape the organization of daily life? Think about the differences between a coal-heated home, a gas-powered kitchen, and an electrified suburb.
Cowan argues that domestic technologies created more work for women, not less. How does this complicate the idea that energy technologies are inherently liberating?
The automobile and petroleum reshaped cities, economies, and geopolitics. As we now face an energy transition away from carbon, what can the history of earlier energy transitions teach us? Is there anything in the readings that suggests these transitions are straightforward – or that they are not?

title: Assignment 2: Article Review
text: Use the research and bibliographic skills you developed in assignment 1 to write a short (3pp, ~800 words) essay engaging with a course reading. Develop an argument, give convincing evidence, and cite at least two additional scholarly sources which also cite the article you have chosen.

title: Assignment 2: Article Review
text: Use the research and bibliographic skills you developed in assignment 1 to write a short (3pp, ~800 words) essay engaging with a course reading. Develop an argument, give convincing evidence, and cite at least two additional scholarly sources which also cite the article you have chosen.

title: Assignment 2: Article Review
text:
Details
This is a lightly-modified “reading response paper”. Your job is to respond to an author’s arguments, either by disagreeing with a point they make, or by extending or enhancing their argument, or, sometimes, all of the above. For this particular assignment, you should also engage with additional sources by authors already engaged in a conversation with the source you are reading.
Your essay should present a clear and compelling argument about the strengths and/or weaknesses of the original source. Your argument should be supported by evidence. Present evidence from both the original reading, and the additional sources you are using. Write in a clear style, and bear in mind the intellectual frameworks that you have learned thus far in the course.
Use Modern Language Association (9th edition) for all citations and bibliography.

title: Assignment 2: Article Review
text:
Readings
Respond to one of the following readings in your essay:

(Marx 2010)
(Franklin 1999)
(Gabrial 2023)
“Landscaping the World”, in (Scott 2017)
(Thompson 1967)

Your additional sources should cite the reading you choose. A Google Scholar citation search can help you in your search.

title: Assignment 1: Working With Sources
text:
Librarian Whitney Kemble has graciously made a pair of videos for us about the Zotero reference management software. The importance of this tool should not be underestimated. Using it will save you many hours – many panicked, late-night hours – over the course of your University studies.
The videos are here:

Video 1
Video 2

You will need to login with your UTORid in order to watch them.
More valuable resources on research skills can be found in these search tutorials and the History research guide. You will likely find these valuable for this assignment a well as subsequent ones.

title: Assignment 1: Working With Sources
text:
Description
In this assignment, you will use the bibliographic skills demonstrated in the videos to locate five scholarly sources. You will then write a single, coherent paragraph in which you cite all five sources, and include a Bibliography with full bibliographic information for each source.
The sources and the paragraph should be relevant to one of the course lectures you have heard. Your choices are:

What is Technology?
Ancient Agriculture
Technologies of Ancient Warfare
The Writing on the Wall
Technologies of the Sea
Technologies of Administration

title: Assignment 1: Working With Sources
text:
Criteria

Your sources should all be scholarly sources. For a definition of a scholarly source, see this link. They should also be relevant to the subject matter at hand, and must not already be in the group Zotero library for this class.
Your paragraph should reference all five sources and very briefly summarize their contributions to your understanding of the topic. It should be clear, concise, and demonstrate your grasp of the subject matter. Length: ~150 +/- 50 w0rds.
Your citations and bibliography should use either the MLA or the Chicago (Notes) style. You should use Zotero to format them perfectly. No partial credit is given for this part of the assignment.

title: Assignment 3: Technology Timeline
text: Choose an everyday technology that forms part of your daily life. It can be ubiquitous (forks, laptop computers, paper clips) or rare. Using TimelineJS, create a timeline identifying important dates in this technology’s history. Then write a brief (900-1100 word) essay about that history.

title: Assignment 3: Technology Timeline
text: Choose an everyday technology that forms part of your daily life. It can be ubiquitous (forks, laptop computers, paper clips) or rare. Using TimelineJS, create a timeline identifying important dates in this technology’s history. Then write a brief (900-1100 word) essay about that history.

title: Assignment 3: Technology Timeline
text:
Details
This is a short research paper accompanied by a simple visualization. Let’s talk about the research paper first, and then about the visualization.
A research paper is a chance to make and support an argument about a historical phenomenon. Your paper should express – and support! – a point of view about its subject. Why is this technology interesting? What “affordances” does it have – what kinds of action does it encourage or discourage? You will need to answer questions like these. While brief, your paper should have an introduction, a conclusion, and several body paragraphs, each of which makes a point and provides evidence for it.
Research Requirements:

Use at least 5 scholarly sources (academic books, journal articles, or equivalent)
Sources must be relevant to your technology’s history and cultural impact
Go beyond basic encyclopedia entries or Wikipedia for your core evidence
Consider primary sources where available (patents, advertisements, user manuals, etc.)

Choosing a Technology Think about the technologies in your life. Where do they come form? What effects do they have on your life? What broader effects have they had in the world? Make a list of two or three or five or ten that are of interest to you. [note: doubtless it is frustrating that I am not listing anything specific. But if I do, then 50% of you will write about one of my examples.]
Then confirm that there is extant scholarly literature about the history of the technologies you are considering. Skim a few articles. Soon you will find some facts and arguments that genuinely interest you. This is a sign that the topic is a good match for you; choose that subject.
Timeline Requirements: TimelineJS is a simple tool for making timelines. Before starting, think about this “information technology” and its role in transmitting/enhancing an argument. Your timeline should:

Contain at least 8 entries spanning your technology’s development
Include pictures in at least 5 entries (ensure images use Creative Commons licensing)
Support and complement your essay’s argument
The library has kindly provided a timeline module here which contains many details about timelines, including helpful guidelines about fair use of images. (Quercus link unavailable during outage) has specific instructions about how to use TimelineJS. Read this carefully. Please note: this module may be revised slightly in the next day or two.

Format Requirements:

Citations in Chicago style (either Notes-Bibliography or Author-Date)
Include a complete bibliography
Submit both your essay and a link to your published TimelineJS timeline

title: Assignment 3: Technology Timeline
text:
Rubric
The Rubric below is provided as an approximate guide. Your project’s final grade may deviate slightly from the percentages assigned in the rubric, especially in cases of unusual excellence or deficit in one or another category.

title: Participation
text:
The participation mark in this class is based on two criteria:

attendance at tutorial
synchronous and asynchronous contributions to the intellectual life of the class

Your TA is in charge of participation marks, and may provide further information beyond what is written here.

title: Participation
text:
The participation mark in this class is based on two criteria:

attendance at tutorial
synchronous and asynchronous contributions to the intellectual life of the class

Your TA is in charge of participation marks, and may provide further information beyond what is written here.

title: Participation
text:
Details
Participation is more than attendance. It involves contributing to the intellectual development of the class. There are many ways to do this, and not all will be fully captured in this mark. However, your engagement in tutorial section, whether by spoken contributions, Zoom chat, or short writing exercises, is an important participation marker. Your TA will say more about the ways in which you can contribute in your particular section.

title: Week 4
text:
This is a big week. We begin with a close reading exercise: Chapter 13, Footnote 4: How to Think Like A Historian, which uses a single footnote in Karl Marx’s Capital to explore how historians build arguments from textual evidence. Then we turn to Machines, Freedom and Unfreedom, exploring the relationship between technology, slavery, and liberation. On Wednesday, we begin with the Midterm Exam. Finally, we pivot to the twentieth century with Media and Mass Culture from Photographs to Television.
You will also have Tutorial 4, which focusses on technologies of mass culture.

title: Week 4
text:
Understanding the Readings

Chapter 13, Footnote 4: How to Think Like a Historian
In Chapter 13 of Capital, Karl Marx writes a famous footnote in which he compares the history of technology to Darwin’s account of the history of natural organs. This lecture takes that single footnote as a starting point for exploring how historians read, interpret, and build arguments from primary sources. We will also read a delightful essay by Margaret Fay that traces the tangled history of the supposed relationship between Marx and Darwin – a story that turns out to be partly a myth constructed from a misattributed letter.
Key readings:

(Marx 2024), Chapter 13, Footnote 4
(Fay and Aveling 1980)



Machines, Freedom and Unfreedom
We will explore the relationship between technology and freedom. In the nineteenth century, technology was described both as a liberator and as an agent of “enslavement.” At the same time, great transformations took place in the age-old practice of human slavery. We will explore the potential of technology to erode inequality, and also its capacity to strengthen the bonds of servitude. Our starting point will be Eli Whitney’s cotton gin, an invention deeply associated with the intensification of slavery in the American South in this period.

title: Week 4
text:
Midterm Exam Preparation
Your midterm exam will cover all material from Weeks 1-3 and the Marx Footnote lecture. Review the following key concepts:

Definitions of technology (Marx, Franklin, Ede)
Agricultural revolution and James C. Scott’s critique
Ancient warfare and military technologies
Writing systems and their social functions
Maritime technologies and navigation
Administrative technologies and colonial power
Time, work discipline, and industrial capitalism
Steam engines and the transformation of labor
Railways, telegraphs, and spatial relationships
Marx’s methodology and the Darwin connection

Be prepared to analyze primary sources and connect technological developments to broader social and cultural changes.

Media and Mass Culture
After the midterm, we explore how photography, radio, and early television transformed human communication and culture. The focus will be on how these technologies created new forms of mass experience and political power.
Key questions to consider:

How did mechanical reproduction change the nature of images and information?
What role did radio play in the rise of fascist movements in the 1930s?
How do new media technologies reshape social relationships and political possibilities?
What is the relationship between technological innovation and cultural change?

The readings will help you understand how 20th-century media technologies fundamentally altered human experience, creating possibilities for both liberation and control that continue to shape our world today.

title: Week 4
text:
Tutorial 4 Preparation
Tutorial 4 focusses on technologies of mass culture. Come prepared to discuss the media readings and their connections to the broader themes of the course.

title: Week 7
text:
In our final week we turn to the questions most commonly associated with the word “technology” in the present moment. First, What is Artificial Intelligence? explores the ideological and technical origins of AI, asking what the term actually means and who benefits from its current deployment. Then, Futures Past and Present closes the course by looking at how past societies imagined technological futures – and what those visions can teach us about navigating the present.
There is no tutorial this week.

title: Week 7
text:
Understanding the Readings

What is Artificial Intelligence?
We will read (Gebru and Torres 2024), which traces the ideological roots of contemporary AI discourse to a cluster of related movements (transhumanism, extropianism, singularitarianism, and others). As you read, consider: what assumptions about human nature and technological progress underlie the AI industry’s self-presentation? How does this connect to earlier themes in the course about technology, power, and social organization?


Futures Past and Present
Our closing lecture asks how visions of the future have changed over time, and what they reveal about the societies that produced them. We will read:

“Introduction: Beyond Scarcity” in (Klein and Thompson 2025)
(Le Guin 1989)

Klein and Le Guin offer very different perspectives on what it means to imagine the future. As you read, think about: what role does technology play in utopian and dystopian visions? How do the historical patterns we have studied all semester shape what futures seem possible – or desirable?

title: Week 2
text:
This week we cover four topics that build a bridge from the ancient world towards the modern period. We begin with Technologies of the Sea, exploring the remarkable navigational traditions of several different maritime cultures. Then we turn to The Writing on the Wall, an exploration of writing as a technology of knowledge and power. Next comes Technologies of Administration, extending the conversation about writing to the tools of governance and census-taking. Finally, we leap forward to explore Time as a Technology, asking how the measurement and regulation of time transformed labour, culture, and daily life.
You will also have Tutorial 2, which will focus on writing technology and the use of Zotero.

title: Week 2
text:
Tutorial 2 Preparation
Be sure to have reviewed (Quercus link unavailable during outage) before tutorial, as well as the readings you have not yet discussed in class. This is also a good opportunity to join the course Zotero Group.

title: Week 2
text:
Understanding the Readings

Technologies of the Sea

Brief sections on navigation from (Headrick 2009) (pp. 43-48, 71-76)
(Walker 2012)
Video Intro: Prof. Rockel

Marine technology is a vast topic. The selection from Headrick serves as a good general introduction to the importance of ship technology over the long term. Walker’s article highlights some of the radical differences between European and Polynesian navigation techniques in the 18th century. My conversation with Prof. Rockel focusses on the remarkable artifacts and skills that made navigation of the Indian Ocean possible in an era long before Europeans and Mediterranean sailors had mastered the techniques of sailing far from shore. As you read and watch, ask yourselves the following questions:

How do bodies of water both divide and connect peoples and states?
Merchant ships and warships have quite distinctive designs. Why is this?
Walker has a lengthy discussion of navigation, and uses the terms “Cartesian coordinates” and “polar coordinates”. Do you understand what these terms mean? How do they differ?
Polynesian and European navigators used very different techniques to solve the same problem (locating ships in the ocean, and using that location to arrive at a destination). What does the difference between these navigation systems teach us about how human beings develop systems to solve important practical problems?



The Writing on the Wall

(Gabrial 2023)

We have an excellent video introduction to the history of writing, from Prof. Suleyman Dost (link here).
While the reading briefly covers the very long history of writing, lecture will focus mostly on early developments. As you read and listen, think about these questions:

Why do societies develop writing? What social needs does it fill?
How do different writing tools and technologies affect what writing can do?
Prof. Dost suggests that the printing press was not as important an advancement as it is often described to be. Can you summarize his argument? Do you agree or disagree?



Technologies of Administration

(Cohn 1987)

You will likely find Cohn’s article somewhat difficult to read. It is organized into four parts:

an unnamed introduction, which introduces a number of difficult terms
An exploration of the pre-history of the census
A discussion of the census itself
A conclusion that summarizes and expands the main arguments.

The arguments here are complex and at times quite difficult. You will have to read carefully and likely re-read. A summary might be something like: in India, during the time of the British Empire, surveys were part of a complex administrative apparatus and also part of a transformation in culture. They were a tool through which the empire understood India, but also a mechanism through which Indians understood themselves and each other. The self-understanding which was generated through the census persisted into the late twentieth century and likely to the present day. We cannot separate techniques of control of populations and the exercise of power from techniques of self-understanding.
Some key terms used here:

colonialism
colonized
culture
rationality
objectification

Prof. Rothman and I discuss these ideas in our interview; you may find it helpful to watch the interview before doing the reading.

Video Intro: Prof. Rothman

As you read, ask yourselves:

What does Cohn mean by “objectification”? Who is doing it, and to whom?
Cohn argues that categories like caste and religion were partly made by the census, not just measured by it. What’s an example of this from his article? Do you find his argument convincing?
How is the census different from earlier forms of writing-based administration? What new kinds of power does enumeration give the state?



Time as a Technology
We make a somewhat sudden jump into the “modern” period – that is, the period beginning some time in the 18th century, in which the basic political, economic, and technological infrastructure that dominate the world today took shape. This lecture concerns the measurement and experience of time. We will read a classic essay on the experience of time focussing on England, and somewhat late in the day (on Tuesday) I will also share a conversation between myself and Prof. Maunaguru about time and the British Empire.
Key readings:

James Patterson testimony, in (Alfred Kydd 1857, 282–84)
(Zerubavel 1982)
Concluding section of (Thompson 1967, 41–43)

As you read, work through these questions:

Thompson draws a sharp line between task-oriented and time-oriented labour. What does each mean, and why does he argue that the shift from one to the other was such a profound rupture in everyday human experience?
The Patterson testimony is a short fragment of primary-source evidence from an English factory worker. Read it carefully and pay attention to the time-related language he uses (and to the factory clock that conditioned his life). What does this brief text tell us about how clock-time was experienced from below – and how does it support or complicate Thompson’s argument?
Zerubavel asks how time itself became standardized. What social and technological forces made this possible? Why is uniform, abstract clock-time – the kind we now take for granted – historically unusual rather than natural?

title: Week 1
text:
Welcome to the course! This first week is packed: we begin with an introduction to the class and its main ideas, then lay a philosophical foundation for thinking about technology and history. From there, we plunge into the ancient world, exploring the agricultural revolution and the technologies of ancient warfare. You will also have your first tutorial at the end of the week.
There are no readings for the first hour, but the remaining lectures have substantial readings. The “What is Technology?” readings are foundational for the course, so read them carefully – you will discuss them in Tutorial 1.

title: Week 1
text:
Readings: What is Technology?

“Introduction” in (Ede 2019)
(Marx 2010) (Excerpts: p.576-77)
“Chapter 1” in (Franklin 1999, 11–34)

All can be found in the Files section of the course site, in the folder “(reading PDF — access instructions sent over email)”. There is a subfolder for each week.
Each of the authors offers a distinctive approach to understanding technology. As you read, try to answer the following questions:

Leo Marx (not to be confused with the much more famous Karl Marx) claims that technology is a “hazardous concept”. What does he mean by this? If the concept is dangerous, can we still use it?
Ursula Franklin refers to technology as having “built the house in which we all live.” What does this phrase mean? She goes on to say she studies “technology as practice,” and to make a distinction between “holistic” and “prescriptive” technologies. Read the essay carefully so that you could explain what it means for technology to be “practice”, and distinguish between “holistic” and “prescriptive” technologies.
Andrew Ede describes technology as “the system by which we attempt to solve real-world problems.” How does thinking about technology as a “system” change his approach to the history of technology? Is this definition surprising? Do you find it convincing? What are “real-world problems” and is there any other kind of problem?

title: Week 1
text:
Tutorial 1 Preparation
The tutorial will be organized around the three “What is Technology?” readings listed above:

“Introduction” in (Ede 2019)
(Marx 2010) (Excerpts: p.576-77)
“Chapter 1” in (Franklin 1999, 11–34)

All can be found in the Files section of the course site, in the folder “(reading PDF — access instructions sent over email)”.
Use the study questions above to prepare.

title: Week 1
text:
Understanding the Readings

Ancient Agriculture
The “Agricultural Revolution” which produced the earliest societies based on a sedentary relationship to arable land, marked a dramatid transformation in the history of our species. With the anthropologist and historian James C. Scott, we explore this shift.
Scott positions himself as a kind of dissident; rather than seeing agriculture as “progress”, he regards it as a tradeoff with profound negative ocnsequences for our ancestors. As you read, try to understand (a) what Scott is arguing against, and (b) what he is arguing for.


Ancient Warfare
Another major development in the ancient world was a series of changes in the pracice of organized collective violence. This is an enormous topic on which we can touch only briefly, but we have the great privilege of an introduction to this area of learning through an interview with Dr. Kate Cooper, which you can watch here:

Prof. K. Cooper

I encourage you all to watch it before the warfare lecture.

title: Syllabus
text:
Warning



Important: The syllabus has now been finalized, but some details of the course delivery are still undetermined.
In particular, we currently plan to require students to use the Respondus LockDown Browser, but this plan may still change before the midterm. We advise you to read the Student Installation Guide and install the browser. Running the browser is a more elaborate process than you are likely to be used to, so ensure you clear some time to learn how to use it. Students should be sure that their devices meet minimum requirements for its usage, and should install the tool before the course begins. You should test the browser well in advance of the midterm, as installation and activation can significantly disrupt your normal laptop usage, and you should avoid any technical issues that might arise on the day. More links and further details will follow closer to the start of class.
We do not currently intend to use iclicker cloud.
Before emailing me, please read the syllabus carefully and consult your TA with questions. Requests for extensions and discussion of accommodations should be directed to your TA. In the event of an irresolvable grading dispute, you may contact me by email. For any matter that does not involve a serious breach of ethics on the part of your TA or other class member, please CC your TA on your message. Whenever contacting me, please use my University email, being sure to put “HISA02” in the subject line.
The thick blue headlines below are “accordions” – click on the headline to show (or rehide) the syllabus sections!

title: Syllabus
text:
Accommodations
Students with diverse learning styles and needs are welcome in this course. In particular, if you have a disability/health consideration that may require accommodations, please feel free to approach me and/or the AccessAbility Services Office as soon as possible.
AccessAbility Services staff (located in Rm AA142, Arts and Administration Building) are available by appointment to assess specific needs, provide referrals and arrange appropriate accommodations 416-287-7560 or email ability.utsc@utoronto.ca. The sooner you let us know your needs the quicker we can assist you in achieving your learning goals in this course.

title: Syllabus
text:
Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion
Diverse backgrounds, embodiments, and experiences are essential to the critical thinking endeavor at the heart of higher education. As always, I expect you to be respectful of the many social and cultural differences among us, which may include, but are not limited to: age, cultural background, disability, ethnicity, technical ability, gender identity and presentation, citizenship and immigration status, national origin, race, religious and political beliefs, sex, sexual orientation, and socioeconomic status. Please talk with me right away if you experience disrespect in this class—from any source, including me—and I will actively work to address the issue.

title: Syllabus
text:
Academic Integrity
The University treats cases of cheating and plagiarism very seriously. The University of Toronto’s Code of Behaviour on Academic Matters outlines the behaviours that constitute academic dishonesty and the processes for addressing academic offences.
Potential offences in papers and assignments include using someone else’s ideas or words without appropriate acknowledgement, submitting your own work in more than one course without the permission of the instructor, making up sources or facts, obtaining or providing unauthorized assistance on any assignment (including from AI agents).
On tests and exams, cheating includes using or possessing unauthorized aids, looking at someone else’s answers during an exam or test, misrepresenting your identity, or falsifying or altering any documentation required by the University.

title: Syllabus
text:
University Land Acknowledgement
We meet on a territory whose history stretches back long before European settlement. For thousands of years, the land on which the University finds itself, as well as greater territory stretching northward from the shores of Lake Ontario, has been the traditional land of Indigenous peoples including the Huron-Wendat, the Seneca, and the Mississaugas of the Credit. Today, this meeting place is still the home to many Indigenous people from across Turtle Island, as well as many newcomers from around the world. We are grateful to have the opportunity to work here, and acknowledge the long history of injustice and expropriation which has brought us to this moment in time.

title: Syllabus
text:
Use of Generative AI Tools
Students may not use artificial intelligence tools for taking tests, writing research papers, or completing major course assignments, except when the professor has explicitly given guidance on the appropriate role of generative AI in that assignment. These tools may be useful when gathering information from across sources and assimilating it for understanding. Each assignment includes detailed instructions regarding the use of generative AI tools. Whenever such tools are used, that use must be acknowledged and described in detail.
The knowing use of generative artificial intelligence tools, including ChatGPT and other AI writing and coding assistants, for the completion of, or to support the completion of, an examination, term test, assignment, or any other form of academic assessment, may be considered an academic offense in this course when that use contravenes my instructions.

title: Syllabus
text:
Attendance
Make every effort to attend each class meeting! Class will begin and end on time. Please do your best to arrive before the start of the session. Students are expected to attend all meetings unless unavoidable circumstances prevent you. Please let me know in advance if you need to skip class either once or in general. I may ask you to make up your attendance mark with extra work.
Please silence notifications on your devices during class, so you can focus. Please don’t use class time (lecture or lab) to check your email, update your status, read reddit, watch YouTube, make dank memes, seize the means of production, etc. Such usage is distracting and interferes with learning both for you and for all the other students around you. If I notice you doing this, I will call you out in class. Spend class time on class materials. If another student’s activity is distracting, please ask them to stop it (or let me know outside of class).

title: Syllabus
text:
Plagiarism Detection Tool
Normally, students will be required to submit their course essays to the University’s plagiarism detection tool for a review of textual similarity and detection of possible plagiarism. In doing so, students will allow their essays to be included as source documents in the tool’s reference database, where they will be used solely for the purpose of detecting plagiarism. The terms that apply to the University’s use of this tool are described on the Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation web site (https://uoft.me/pdt-faq).

title: Syllabus
text:
Video Recording and Sharing
This course, including your participation, will be recorded on video and will be available to students in the course for viewing remotely and after each session.
Course videos and materials belong to your instructor, the University, and/or other sources depending on the specific facts of each situation and are protected by copyright. In this course, you are permitted to download session videos and materials for your own academic use, but you should not copy, share, or use them for any other purpose without the explicit permission of the instructor.
For questions about the recording and use of videos in which you appear, please contact your instructor.

title: Syllabus
text:
Missed Deadlines or Exams
Life happens. If illness, family emergency, or other serious circumstances prevent you from meeting a deadline or writing the midterm, contact me (or your TA, for assignments) as soon as possible — ideally before the deadline. Do not wait until after the fact.
Assignments. Requests for extensions should be made in advance where possible. Short extensions (1–3 days) can usually be arranged without formal documentation if you reach out promptly. Longer extensions may require supporting documentation (see below). Work submitted late without an approved extension is subject to the 5%/calendar-day late penalty described below.
Declaring an Absence. For a missed assignment deadline or the midterm, your first step is normally to file an Absence Declaration on ACORN. Students may use the ACORN Absence Declaration tool once per academic term to declare an absence of up to seven consecutive days without submitting documentation. See the UTSC Registrar’s Absence Declaration page for details. You must also notify me by email within 48 hours so we can make a plan.
If you have already used your ACORN Absence Declaration for the term, or if your circumstances fall outside what ACORN covers, you may be asked to submit a Verification of Illness or Injury (VOI) form or other appropriate documentation.
Midterm. The midterm is written in class on May 27, 2026 (Wed). If you miss it with an approved absence, the weight of the midterm will normally be shifted to the final exam; a make-up midterm will not typically be offered.
Final Exam. Missed final exams are handled through the Office of the Registrar’s deferred exam process, not by the instructor. If you miss the final, you must petition the Registrar for a deferred exam sitting.
Accommodated students. Students registered with AccessAbility Services should arrange test and exam accommodations through that office following the usual procedures; please also let me know in advance.

title: Syllabus
text:
Health & Wellness
The University is committed to promoting the health and wellbeing of all members of our community. Beginning with the Healthy Campus Initiative hub online, there are a variety of resources on campus to support faculty, staff, and students in this regard, including:

Campus Safety Operations – Provides advice and consultation on matters of student behavior. Is also responsible for UTSC Community Police services, UTSC Parking Services and the UTSC Emergency Medical Response Group.
The Health and Wellness Centre – The Health & Wellness Centre provides a full range of general health services, including diagnostic, treatment and referral services for illnesses ranging from the medical to psychological.
Sexual Violence Prevention and Support Centre (U of T) – The Sexual Violence Prevention and Support Centre works to create a campus environment where all members of the University community can study, work and live free from sexual violence. The Centre has locations on each campus to help students, staff and faculty who have been affected by sexual violence or sexual harassment access support, services and accommodations. At UTSC the Centre is located in the Environmental Science & Chemistry Building, EV141.
UTSC Mental Health Network – The UTSC Mental Health Network is an organization of students, staff, and faculty, which developed this list of resources to navigate mental health services on and off campus.
